text: In Poland, standard onboarding requires a signed employment contract, proof of identity, social security number (PESEL) or equivalent, and bank account details for salary payment.
German onboarding includes preparation of employment contract, registration with health insurance, and provision of tax identification number.
French onboarding typically involves issuing a written contract, informing social security authorities, and providing required statutory disclosures to the employee.
In the UK, onboarding requires issuing a statement of terms and conditions, registering employees for tax, and setting up payroll.
Spanish onboarding includes signing the contract, registering with social security, and ensuring all work permits and required documentation are filed.
Italian onboarding procedures require execution of employment contract, social security registration, and submission of safety documentation.
Irish onboarding includes providing a contract, registering for tax registration, and setting up employment records.
Dutch onboarding requires an employment contract, registration of the employment relationship with the tax authorities, and setup of payroll.
Belgian onboarding involves signing employment contract, social security declaration, and registration with relevant labor authorities.
Swedish onboarding requires employment contract, tax registration, and notification to the public employment service where applicable.

In Germany, termination during probation is generally allowed with shorter notice periods, but must not be discriminatory or in breach of special protections such as pregnancy or works council members.
French probationary periods allow simplified termination subject to notice requirements that vary based on seniority and contractual terms.
In the UK, during probation unfair dismissal protections may have longer qualifying periods, but employers must still avoid discriminatory reasons.
Spanish probationary periods must be explicitly documented in the contract and allow simplified dismissal during the trial period.
Poland allows probationary periods that permit simplified termination during the probation, but employer and employee rights must still be observed.
Dutch probation rules allow shorter notice during the trial period but require formal documentation to be enforceable.
Italian probation periods vary by sector and contract type; termination during probation requires proper notice as specified in collective bargaining agreements.
Irish probation allows employers flexibility in termination but does not remove other employment law protections during the trial period.
Belgian employment law allows a trial period during which either party may terminate with proper notice, as specified in contract or law.
Sweden permits trial employment periods where either party may terminate without cause if notice is provided as required.

In France, an employee with two years of continuous service is typically required to give at least one month's notice before resigning, unless otherwise specified in their contract or collective agreement.
German notice periods scale with tenure; during probation notice is shorter, while longer tenures require more formal notice periods defined by law or collective agreements.
In the UK, notice periods depend on contract and statutory minimums; employers typically must provide at least one week's notice, and employees may contractually require longer notice.
Spanish notice periods vary by cause and contract type but generally require statutory notice unless otherwise agreed in contract or collective agreement.
In Italy, notice periods depend on collective bargaining agreements and contract terms, varying significantly by sector and seniority.
Polish notice periods typically range from one to three months depending on seniority and role, as defined by law or contract.
Dutch notice periods are typically one month, but collective agreements or contracts may specify longer periods based on tenure.
Belgian notice periods depend on employment category and tenure, generally ranging from days to months as specified in law or contract.
Irish notice periods are generally a minimum of two weeks under statute, but contracts may specify longer periods.
Swedish notice periods are typically one month and apply to both employer and employee, unless collective agreement specifies otherwise.
German termination procedures require compliance with notice periods and may be subject to protection schemes for certain groups.
French termination procedures must follow statutory requirements and may involve consultation with employee representatives.
In the UK, unfair dismissal protections generally apply after a qualifying period; dismissals must follow fair procedures and have fair reasons.
In Spain, termination procedures must follow statutory notice requirements and may vary by contract type; certain dismissals require a specific cause.
Italian termination is subject to protective rules and may require proper cause documentation, with potential severance obligations.
Polish termination requires adherence to notice periods and may involve severance payments depending on reason and tenure.
Dutch termination processes require notice of intent and may need approval from labor authority in certain cases.
Belgian termination must follow notice requirements; severance obligations vary based on tenure and contract category.
Irish termination is subject to unfair dismissal protections after a qualifying period and must follow fair procedures.
Swedish termination requires just cause unless in probation, with notice and potentially severance obligations.
In the UK, full-time employees are entitled to a minimum of 28 days of paid annual leave (which can include public holidays).
German employees are entitled to a minimum of 20 days of annual leave per year, plus statutory public holidays.
French employees are entitled to five weeks of paid annual leave plus statutory public holidays.
Spanish employees receive a minimum of 30 days of annual leave plus 14 public holidays per year.
Italian employees are entitled to a minimum of 26 days of annual leave plus numerous statutory public holidays.
Polish employees receive minimum 26 days of annual leave plus statutory public holidays.
Dutch employees are entitled to a minimum of 20 days of annual leave based on a 5-day working week.
Belgian employees receive a minimum of 20 days of annual leave plus 10 statutory public holidays.
Irish employees are entitled to a statutory minimum of 3 weeks annual leave and 9 public holidays per year.
Swedish employees receive a minimum of 25 days of paid annual leave plus statutory public holidays.
Portuguese employees are entitled to minimum 22 days of annual leave plus 11 public holidays per year.
Austrian employees are entitled to a minimum of 20 days of annual leave plus numerous public holidays.
Danish employees receive a minimum of 6 weeks of paid annual leave based on collective agreements.
Finnish employees are entitled to a minimum of 20 days of annual leave plus statutory public holidays.
Norwegian employees receive a minimum of 21 days of annual leave plus statutory public holidays.
Greek employees are entitled to 20 days of annual leave plus various public holidays.
Czech employees receive a minimum of 20 days of annual leave plus statutory public holidays.
Hungarian employees are entitled to minimum 20 days of paid annual leave plus public holidays.
Slovak employees receive 20 days of annual leave minimum plus statutory public holidays.
Estonian employees are entitled to minimum 20 days of annual leave plus statutory public holidays.
